metadata.title: Session, Karaoke & Audiogram
metadata.purpose: Video for 'Brutalist — Claude for Video Production' on @NikBearBrown. Teaches three voice-layer tools in one arc: session notes (direct the reading, not just label it), the align.py word clock (faster-whisper timing + SequenceMatcher = every word on its spoken moment), and lyric-overlay (karaoke layer + waveform on a finished video, never a re-render). Byron's She Walks in Beauty — the channel's own session-directed Suno reading from she-walks-in-beauty/pantry/ — threads through as the worked example: the poem opens a section, the tools work on it, the poem closes it.
metadata.clock: narration — the master clock; every beat conforms to its measured audio (Suno path)
metadata.voice_engine: suno (session notes + directed lyrics → human generates → pantry stem → suno-slice)

beats.narration_text: You're about to hear a stanza of Byron — She Walks in Beauty, read in this channel's voice under session-notes direction. That's not a voice actor. That's a directed reading, and the direction is text.
beats.delivery: open direct, unhurried, slight warmth on 'Byron'

production_viz.label: the [spoken word] era — a stop sign, not a direction
production_viz.mechanic: A mock Suno lyrics box: one [spoken word] tag at the very top, then three stanzas of text with no further tags. To the right, a musical note icon creeps into the second stanza despite the style note — Suno decided that line deserved a melody. Caption below: 'a stop sign is not a direction.' Colors: #FFFFFF / #2A1A0E / #C8102E (note in red).

beats.narration_text: The old fix for keeping Suno in spoken word was one line at the top of the lyrics box: spoken word. It stopped the singing. But a stop sign is not a direction. Suno still had to decide how to read — and it guessed.
beats.delivery: dry, slightly deadpan — let 'it guessed' land flat

props.code:
VOICE: Bear Brown (the channel's own voice)
REGISTER: measured, intimate — no melodrama
TEMPO: ~80 wpm — slower than speech, not slower than thought
BED: sparse atmospheric pad, quiet under the reading

BREATH RULE: full silence between every stanza.
The slicer needs the cut. Non-negotiable.

This is a reading, not a performance.
The voice is close, grounded. The poem does the work.

beats.narration_text: Session notes start with the STYLE box — a paste into Suno's STYLE field before the lyrics ever load. Voice, register, tempo, bed guidance, and the one rule the slicer needs: a full breath of silence between every section.
beats.delivery: steady, precise — each component named cleanly

props.code:
[spoken word — hushed, one breath per line, settle on 'night']
She walks in beauty, like the night
Of cloudless climes and starry skies
And all that's best of dark and bright
Meet in her aspect and her eyes

[spoken word — warmer, let 'tender' soften — no rush on 'grace']
Thus mellowed to that tender light
Which heaven to gaudy day denies
One shade the more, one ray the less
Had half impaired the nameless grace

beats.narration_text: Then the lyrics box: the poem, with one direction tag above each stanza. Not a style label — a producer's note. Hushed, one breath per line, settle on night. The voice has something to aim at. This is the actual session that produced the She Walks in Beauty reading.
beats.delivery: warm, slightly slower — let the producer-note example land before moving on

production_viz.label: the word clock — known text + whisper timing = exact placement
production_viz.mechanic: Three-column diagram. LEFT: the known narration text ('She / walks / in / beauty…') stacked vertically, labeled KNOWN TEXT. CENTER: faster-whisper output — timestamps in teal (0.17s / 0.42s / 0.51s / 0.60s…), labeled TIMING. RIGHT: words.json output — {text: 'She', startFrame: 4, endFrame: 9}, each matched word in teal. An arrow from LEFT+CENTER → RIGHT with label 'SequenceMatcher'. Caption: 'text already known → timing, not transcription.'

beats.narration_text: Now the karaoke piece. The text is already known — it's what you wrote, or in this case, the exact poem. So align dot py never transcribes blind. It runs faster-whisper for timing, then SequenceMatcher maps the known words onto those timestamps. Every word, its real moment.
beats.delivery: steady, plainspoken, dry — matter-of-fact about the logic

beats.narration_text: One command: python scripts align dot py, the reel folder, optional model flag. It writes words dot json — frame-level start and end for every word in every beat. Three things read it: the Remotion karaoke plane, the caption publisher, and the audiogram.
Applied to the Byron excerpt, this is what comes back: She at frame four. Walks at ten. Every word placed on the moment it was spoken — matched, not guessed.
beats.delivery: slightly warmer — a small reveal; let the individual word placements land

production_viz.label: audiogram layers — decoration on a finished frame
production_viz.mechanic: An exploded layer stack, bottom to top: (1) SOURCE VIDEO FRAME — a Byron-era illustration or dark still, labeled 'already rendered'; (2) OSCILLOSCOPE WAVEFORM — a teal sine-like bar across the mid-frame; (3) KARAOKE WORD LAYER — 'She walks in beauty' with the current word bright and the rest dim; (4) AUDIO — the same track extracted from the video (dashed line up from the source frame to the waveform). Caption: 'decorate the finished frame — never re-render.'

beats.narration_text: Now the audiogram. Lyric overlay starts with a finished video and puts two things on top: a karaoke word layer and an oscilloscope waveform. Never a re-render. The picture exists — this is just decoration, landing clean.

Overlay new dot py takes a source video and a lyrics file. Step one: extract audio from the video — so waveform and picture can never drift, because they're the same recording. Step two: stamp a Remotion project that places the word layer on the waveform band.
beats.delivery: steady, plainspoken — the two-step logic is the point

production_viz.label: the audiogram on Byron — hero beat
production_viz.mechanic: Hero moment — most visual care. Dark background #2A1A0E. Center: a teal oscilloscope waveform bar pulsing across the full width (simulated with a sine-like animation). Below the waveform, in EB Garamond white at 80px: 'SHE WALKS IN BEAUTY'. In smaller teal below: 'ONE COMMAND  ·  ONE EXTRACT  ·  ONE RENDER'. The waveform pulses with the rhythm of the reading. Caption omitted — the visual is the statement.

beats.narration_text: The Byron reading with the overlay: the waveform pulses on her voice, and each word appears at the exact moment it lands. One command from a finished clip — one render — a complete audiogram.
beats.delivery: slower, weighted — let each phrase land; this is the payoff

production_viz.label: poem vs promo — the restraint choice — hero beat
production_viz.mechanic: Hero split — most care. Dark background #2A1A0E. Vertical divider down the center. LEFT half: label 'POEM' in teal, then in white: 'burned-in words / compete with listening' — below in red: 'you cannot be moved / and read at the same time'. RIGHT half: label 'PROMO AUDIOGRAM' in teal, then in white: 'words / are the visual' — below in teal: 'there is no film / to protect'. Bottom center in white, full width: 'THE MACHINE SYNCS EVERY WORD — YOU DECIDE WHAT THE VIEWER READS'.

beats.narration_text: Here is the choice. On a poem, burned-in words compete with the act of listening. You cannot be moved by verse and read it at the same time. On a promo audiogram, the words are the visual — there is no film to protect. The machine syncs every word. You decide what the viewer reads.
beats.delivery: weighted, measured — each clause lands separately; pause before 'You decide'

props.command: $ # the voice chain — three tools, one sentence
output: the full chain — direction to delivery to display

beats.narration_text: Three tools, one sentence: session directs the voice, karaoke clocks the words, audiogram makes sound visible. The full chain — from direction to delivery to display — is now yours.
beats.delivery: conclusive, slightly warmer — the student has what they came for

beats.narration_text: That's the voice chain. Subscribe to follow the series, and find the whole toolkit at www dot brutalist dot art.
